elements.sent-2411-001: We construct a new family of quantum chaotic models by combining multiple copies of integrable commuting SYK models.
elements.sent-2411-002: As each copy of the commuting SYK model does not commute with others, this construction breaks the integrability of each commuting SYK and the family of models demonstrates the emergence of quantum chaos.
elements.sent-2411-003: We study the spectrum of this model analytically in the double-scaled limit.
elements.sent-2411-004: As the number of copies tends to infinity, the spectrum becomes compact and equivalent to the regular SYK model.
elements.sent-2411-005: For finite $d$ copies, the spectrum is close to the regular SYK model in UV but has an exponential tail $e^{E/T_c}$ in the IR.
elements.sent-2411-006: We identify the reciprocal of the exponent in the tail as a critical temperature $T_c$, above which the model should be quantum chaotic.
elements.sent-2411-007: We propose the existence of a new phase around $T_c$, and the dynamics should be very different in two phases.
elements.sent-2411-008: We further carry out numeric analysis at finite $d$, which supports our proposal.
elements.sent-2411-009: Given any finite dimensional local Hamiltonian, by decomposing it into $d$ groups, in which all terms in one group commute with each other but terms from different groups may not, our analysis can give an estimate of the critical temperature for quantum chaos based on the decomposition.
elements.sent-2411-010: We also comment on the implication of the critical temperature to future quantum simulations of quantum chaos and quantum gravity.
elements.sent-2411-011: One interesting question regarding holography is to understand how holography could happen in a quantum system.
elements.sent-2411-012: Since the born of AdS/CFT, people have found various models that exhibit holographic features.
elements.sent-2411-013: Besides these explicit constructions, a universal microscopic mechanism that achieves holography is still under exploration.
elements.sent-2411-014: In recent years, people discovered the SYK model \cite{sachdev1993gapless,kitaev2015simple,Maldacena:2016hyu as one of the simplest toy models with holographic features.}
elements.sent-2411-015: It is a quantum mechanical model of $N$ Majorana fermions in 0+1 dimension with random $q$-local Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-016: Due to its simple structure, we may be able to examine how its holography emerges in much detail.
elements.sent-2411-017: One signature of holography in the SYK model is the quantum chaos \cite{Shenker:2013pqa,Shenker:2014cwa,Roberts:2014isa,kitaev2015simple,Maldacena:2016hyu , which is dual to the boost symmetry of the dual AdS$_2$ black hole spacetime near the horizon.}
elements.sent-2411-018: In low temperatures, the SYK model has an effective action of Schwarzian derivative, which coincides with the boundary description of the reparameterization modes \cite{Maldacena:2016hyu of the Jackiw–Teitelboim gravity with negative cosmological constant \cite{Teitelboim:1983ux,Jackiw:1984je .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: The partition function from the Schwarzian derivative is 1-loop exact and leads to the IR spectrum $\r(E)\sim \sinh C\sqrt{E-E_0}$ for some $C>0$ \cite{Stanford:2017thb .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The $e^{C\sqrt{E-E_0 }$ scaling in IR coincides with the spectrum of microscopic states of a black hole, and suggests a holographic interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Moreover, a finite $E_0$ implies a lower bound for black hole energy.
elements.sent-2411-022: As Schwarzian derivative leads to exponential growth $e^{\lam t}$ in out-of-time-ordered correlation (OTOC) with $\lam=1$ \cite{Maldacena:2016hyu , this spectrum is strongly related to the maximal quantum chaos \footnote{$\lam$ is the quantum Lyapunov exponent describing how fast the local information is scrambled over the system.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: There is a theorem \cite{Maldacena:2015waa giving an upper bound $\lambda\leq 1$ due to unitarity, and thus $\lam=1$ is the maximal chaos, which is saturated by gravity.. .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Another example of a bounded continuous spectrum in the ensemble average is the random matrix theory (e.g. GUE ensemble).
elements.sent-2411-025: If we regard each sample of the random hermitian matrices as the Hamiltonian of some system, the dynamics are clearly chaotic.
elements.sent-2411-026: Indeed, it has been discovered that a double-scaled matrix model is dual to JT gravity \cite{Saad:2019lba , which is in spirit similar to the duality between random matrices and string theories \cite{Ginsparg:1993is .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: Furthermore, SYK type of models has another appealing feature, namely they have potential experimental realization in various setups~\cite{Danshita:2016xbo,Garcia-Alvarez:2016wem,PhysRevLett.121.036403,Pikulin:2017mhj,Chew:2017xuo,Luo:2017bno,Brzezinska:2022mhj,Uhrich:2023ddx .}
elements.sent-2411-028: In fact, a very interesting recently proposal~\cite{Jafferis:2022crx claims that on a quantum computer, one can realize a special SYK-like model on a quantum processer and one observe characteristic features of tranversable wormholes in gravity.}
elements.sent-2411-029: However, the model in is actually ``commuting", meaning that all terms in the Hamiltonian mutually commute.
elements.sent-2411-030: As a result the model possesses lots of conserved quantities and cannot be quantum chaotic.
elements.sent-2411-031: The commuting SYK model is further analyzed in~\cite{Gao:2023gta .}
elements.sent-2411-032: As a random but integrable version of the SYK model, it has an unbounded spectrum of Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-033: It has been shown in \cite{Gao:2023gta that this integrable variant does not exhibit chaotic behavior though it has some similarities.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Interestingly, this provides a new angle to explore the mechanism of quantum chaos (or perhaps holography) in the regular SYK model.
elements.sent-2411-035: Indeed, we can decompose the regular SYK Hamiltonian as the sum over multiple groups, each of which contains only mutually commutating terms and thus is integrable, but different groups do not commute with each other.
elements.sent-2411-036: If we imagine the SYK Hamiltonian as the limit of adding these groups one by one, the chaotic nature emerges as we include huge numbers of these groups, and the integrability is dramatically violated.
elements.sent-2411-037: To understand this process from integrability to chaos in more detail, we explicitly constructed $d$ copies of the commuting SYK Hamiltonians $\tilde H_a$ for $a=1,\cdots,d$.
elements.sent-2411-038: For each $\tilde H_a$, we used different commuting basis so that $\tilde H_a$ do not commute with each other.
elements.sent-2411-039: We may use a parameter $q=e^{-\lam}\in[0,1]$ to characterize strength of the non-commutativity between two $\tilde H_a$: if $q\ra 0$ the non-commutativity is extremely strong and $q\ra 1$ means $\tilde H_a$ all commute.
elements.sent-2411-040: The total Hamiltonian is given by \be \tilde{H =\f 1{\sqrt{d }\sum_{a=1 ^{d}\tilde{H _{a \ee Since each $\tilde H_a$ has an ensemble of random couplings, we are interested in the spectrum of $\tilde H$ under ensemble average \be \r(E)=\E \left[\sum_{\tilde\lambda \d(E-\tilde \lambda)\right] \ee where $\tilde \lambda$ is the eigenvalue of $\tilde H$ for each instantiation in the random ensemble.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: For convenience, let us call this model as ``d-commuting SYK model" (dcSYK).
elements.sent-2411-042: We show that the spectrum is non-compact unless $d\ra \infty$.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the latter case, the spectrum (in the double-scaled limit) is identical to the regular SYK model.
elements.sent-2411-044: In particular, there is an emergent finite lower bound of the spectrum $E_0$ as $d\ra\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our model gives a natural interpolation between the integrable $d=1$ commuting SYK model to an equivalently (in the spectrum sense) chaotic SYK model.
elements.sent-2411-046: Tentatively, we regard a compact spectrum with a square-root form of edge $\r(E)\sim \sqrt{E-E_0}$ as a characteristic of quantum chaos as it is the common feature of SYK model and random matrices.
elements.sent-2411-047: For the dcSYK model with finite $d$, the spectrum is close to the $d\ra\infty$ form above some energy scale and has an infinite exponential tail $e^{E/T_c}$ in the low energy regime.
elements.sent-2411-048: Equivalently, for the canonical ensemble of temperature $T$, there is a critical temperature $T_c$ so that we can approximately regard the spectrum as chaotic for $T>T_c$ and non-chaotic for $T<T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-049: We compute $T_c$ in two nontrivial cases and find that it is a monotonically decreasing function of $d$.
elements.sent-2411-050: This shows how the chaotic regime emerges and extends as we include more components $\tilde H_a$ in the Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-051: There is a recent work \cite{Berkooz:2024evs,Berkooz:2024ofm constructing an interpolation between integrability and quantum chaos with a tunable linear combination of the commuting SYK model and the regular SYK model.}
elements.sent-2411-052: They also find a critical temperature separating the integrable and chaotic regimes.
elements.sent-2411-053: Our model is probably a more natural and nontrivial interpolation and shows how quantum chaos emerges as $d$ increases.
elements.sent-2411-054: Moreover, our model has an operational significance in analyzing a generic random model.
elements.sent-2411-055: Following the aforementioned idea of decomposing the regular SYK model into integrable groups, this decomposition can be done for any given local random Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-056: Minimizing the number of groups $d$ in the decomposition, we can compute an averaged non-commutativity parameter $\lam$, and use the dcSYK model to estimate the critical temperature $T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-057: This gives a rapid diagnosis of how chaotic a given model is.
elements.sent-2411-058: In this sense, we propose that our model is a prototype of a universality of random models, which is parameterized by $d$ and $\lam$.

elements.section-2411-004: Coarse-grained path integral for $q$ close to 1
elements.sent-2411-254: In this section, we will study the spectrum, and especially the part near the edge for $q$ close to 1.
elements.sent-2411-255: This limit is specially interesting because for double-scaled SYK model, if we zoom into the edge and take $q\ra 1$, we will find the spectrum has the form of $\sinh C\sqrt{E-E_0}$, which is consistent with the Schwarzian derivative as the IR effective action.
elements.sent-2411-256: For convenience, let us call this limit as the Schwarzian limit from now.
elements.sent-2411-257: This gives a strong evidence that the low temperature double-scaled SYK model with $q\ra 1$ has a dual of JT gravity.
elements.sent-2411-258: As explained in Section \ref{sec:2C , our dcSYK model becomes equivalent to the double-scaled SYK model in $d\ra \infty$ limit.}
elements.sent-2411-259: It is natural to ask how the finite $d$ effect changes the spectrum in the Schwarzian limit.
elements.sent-2411-260: For a finite $d$, each moment of $H$ for $q> 0$ must be greater than that for $q=0$ because each moment is the sum over many chord diagrams and $q>0$ has more diagrams than $q=0$ because the latter disallows intersection between different chords.
elements.sent-2411-261: Since diagrams are evaluated as positive numbers, the $n$-th moment of $q>0$ must grow faster than that of $q=0$, which is already faster than exponentially.
elements.sent-2411-262: By Lemma \ref{lemma3 , we immediately conclude that any finite $d$ dcSYK model must have non-compact spectrum.}
elements.sent-2411-263: Therefore, our focus will be understanding how the finite $d$ effect gives the interplay between the chaotic bounded spectrum of $\sinh C \sqrt{E-E_0}$ and the unbounded tail.
elements.sent-2411-264: Though the exact solution for dcSYK is unavailable, for $q$ close to 1 there is a nice approximate method developed in \cite{Berkooz:2024evs,Berkooz:2024ofm to deal with 2 types of chords in the double-scaled SYK model.}
elements.sent-2411-265: The method is a coarse-grained path integral representation for chord diagrams, and we will slightly improve the form and generalize it to chord diagrams with $d$ colors.
elements.sent-2411-266: We will briefly review the construction of the coarse-grained path integral representation without giving derivation details.

elements.sent-2411-561: In this work, we have studied a variant of the $p$-local SYK model by combining $d$ copies of the integrable commuting SYK models, which we call the d-commuting SYK model.
elements.sent-2411-562: Though all terms in each commuting SYK Hamiltonian are commutative, each commuting SYK Hamiltonian does not commute with each other because they are constructed by different basis.
elements.sent-2411-563: Since the total Hamiltonian is the sum of these commuting SYK components, the integrability breaks down for $d>1$.
elements.sent-2411-564: We study the spectrum of this model in the double-scale limit with $\lam=4p^2/N$ fixed and $N\ra\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-565: The spectrum is non-compact for generic $d$ and becomes compact only when $d\ra\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-566: Regarding its shape, it is close to the compact form of $d\ra \infty$ above some energy scale but has an exponential suppressed tail below it.
elements.sent-2411-567: Therefore, this model can be regarded as an interpolation between integrability and chaos.
elements.sent-2411-568: For the canonical ensemble with temperature $T$, we can regard the system as chaotic for $T>T_c$ and non-chaotic for $T<T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-570: The first case is $\lam\ra\infty$ where we can use free probability to find the exact form of the spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-571: The critical temperature $T_c=1/(2d)$ is read from the exponent of the exponential tail $e^{(E-E_0)/T_c}$ for $E<E_0$ for some $E_0$.
elements.sent-2411-572: The exponential tail is from a non-pertrubative effect in $d$.
elements.sent-2411-573: The second case is $\lam\ra 0$ in the Schwarzian limit where we use the coarse-grained path integral to find the approximate spectrum in the IR.
elements.sent-2411-574: At the 1-loop level, we find the spectrum has an exponential tail for $d<9$.
elements.sent-2411-575: For $d>9$ we speculate the tail is due to higher loop effects.
elements.sent-2411-576: In both cases, we observe $T_c$ decreases as $d$ increases, which indicates a bigger chaotic regime.
elements.sent-2411-577: The thermodynamical meaning of the critical temperature $T_c$ is different for the two cases.
elements.sent-2411-578: For $\lam\ra\infty$, the thermodynamical critical temperature for a phase transition is much lower than $T_c$; for $\lam \ra 0$, it is of the same order as $T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-579: There have been other discussions about integrability to chaos transition in the context of SYK-like models.
elements.sent-2411-580: They generically fall into two classes.
elements.sent-2411-581: One class involves a direct coupling between an integrable model and a chaotic model, and the transition is a direct consequence of the competition between the two constituent models~\cite{Peng:2017kro .}
elements.sent-2411-582: The second class does not have such explicit coupling and the transition is purely due to the strong interaction within the single Hamiltonian~\cite{Peng:2018zap,Chang:2021wbx,Chang:2021fmd and the transition appears at a special value of a parameter of the model.}
elements.sent-2411-583: The theory we consider in this paper is another example of the second class, where the transition appears at $d=1$.
elements.sent-2411-584: To justify the 1-loop computation of the coarse-grained path integral for $d<9$, we simulate the dcSYK model for $N=15$ and $p=2$, and draw the spectrum by direct diagonalization.
elements.sent-2411-585: Fitting the data, we find a nice match of both critical temperature $T_c$ and the ``ground" energy $\tilde E_0$ with the 1-loop computation for $d=2,3$.
elements.sent-2411-586: The fit becomes worse as $d$ gets larger due to both higher loop and the finite $d/N$ corrections.
elements.sent-2411-587: Numeric spectral form factor at different temperatures also demonstrate a strong evidence that the dcSYK model has a phase transition at some temperature $\sim O(T_c)$.
elements.sent-2411-588: The model is quantum chaotic only above this temperature in long time scales.
elements.sent-2411-589: The existence of the critical temperature has a useful implication for future quantum simulation experiments for holographic systems (e.g. SYK model) under the slogan of ``quantum gravity in a lab" initiated by Susskind in \cite{Susskind:2017ney .}
elements.sent-2411-590: However, the regular SYK model has $O(N^p)$ numbers of terms and is extremely hard to implement on a quantum simulator.
elements.sent-2411-591: On the other hand, the sparse SYK model \cite{Xu:2020shn containing randomly picked $O(N)$ terms is much simpler and argued to have a holographic description as well.}
elements.sent-2411-592: Along this line, one of the most interesting simulation projects is the teleportation protocol of traversable wormholes \cite{Gao:2016bin,Gao:2019nyj,Schuster:2021uvg .}

elements.sent-2411-674: One interesting question regarding holography is understanding how holographic duality could emerge in quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-675: Since the birth of AdS/CFT correspondence, researchers have identified various models exhibiting holographic features.
elements.sent-2411-676: Beyond these explicit constructions, a universal microscopic mechanism underlying holography remains under exploration.
elements.sent-2411-677: In recent years, the SYK model~\cite{sachdev1993gapless,kitaev2015simple,Maldacena:2016hyu has emerged as one of the simplest toy models possessing holographic properties.}
elements.sent-2411-678: This quantum mechanical model describes $N$ Majorana fermions in (0+1) dimensions with a random $q$-local Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-679: Owing to its simplified structure, it allows detailed examination of holographic emergence.
elements.sent-2411-680: One signature of holography in the SYK model is quantum chaos~\cite{Shenker:2013pqa,Shenker:2014cwa,Roberts:2014isa,kitaev2015simple,Maldacena:2016hyu,Kobrin:2020xms , which is dual to the boost symmetry of the AdS$_2$ black hole spacetime near the horizon.}
elements.sent-2411-681: At low temperatures, the SYK model has an effective action described by a Schwarzian derivative~\cite{Maldacena:2016upp , matching the boundary description of reparameterization modes~\cite{Maldacena:2016hyu in Jackiw-Teitelboim gravity with negative cosmological constant~\cite{Teitelboim:1983ux,Jackiw:1984je .}}}
elements.sent-2411-682: The partition function derived from the Schwarzian derivative is 1-loop exact and yields the IR spectrum $\rho(E)\sim \sinh C\sqrt{E-E_0}$ for some $C>0$ \cite{Stanford:2017thb .}
elements.sent-2411-683: The $e^{C\sqrt{E-E_0 }$ scaling in the IR regime coincides with the spectrum of microscopic states of a black hole, suggesting a holographic interpretation.}
elements.sent-2411-684: Since the Schwarzian derivative leads to exponential growth $e^{\lambda t}$ in out-of-time-ordered correlators (OTOCs) with $\lambda=1$~\cite{Maldacena:2016hyu , this spectrum is strongly related to maximal quantum chaos\footnote{$\lambda$ represents the quantum Lyapunov exponent characterizing how fast the local information is scrambled over the system.}}
elements.sent-2411-685: A theorem \cite{Maldacena:2015waa establishes an upper bound $\lambda\leq 1$ due to unitarity, with $\lambda=1$ corresponding to maximal chaos – a saturation condition achieved by gravitational systems.. .}
elements.sent-2411-686: Another example of a bounded continuous spectrum in the ensemble average appears in random matrix theory (e.g., the GUE ensemble).
elements.sent-2411-687: Treating each random Hermitian matrix sample as as the Hamiltonian of some system, the system exhibits explicitly chaotic dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-688: Notably, a double-scaled matrix model has been shown dual to JT gravity~\cite{Saad:2019lba , which is in spirit analogous to the duality between random matrices and string theories \cite{Ginsparg:1993is .}}
elements.sent-2411-689: Furthermore, SYK type models possess another appealing feature: potential experimental realizations in various platforms~\cite{Danshita:2016xbo,Garcia-Alvarez:2016wem,PhysRevLett.121.036403,Pikulin:2017mhj,Chew:2017xuo,Luo:2017bno,Brzezinska:2022mhj,Uhrich:2023ddx .}
elements.sent-2411-690: Intriguingly, a recent proposal~\cite{Jafferis:2022crx suggests that certain SYK-like models implemented on quantum processors could exhibit signatures of traversable wormholes.}
elements.sent-2411-691: However, the specific model proposed is "commuting" – all Hamiltonian terms mutually commute – resulting in numerous conserved quantities that preclude quantum chaos.
elements.sent-2411-692: As a random yet integrable SYK variant, it displays an unbounded Gaussian-distributed spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-693: It has been demonstrated in~\cite{Gao:2023gta that this integrable system does not exhibit chaotic behavior despite superficial similarities.}
elements.sent-2411-694: Specifically, we can decompose the regular SYK Hamiltonian into multiple groups of mutually commuting terms (and is thus integrable), but different groups do not commute with each other.
elements.sent-2411-695: To systematically study this integrability-to-chaos transition, we explicitly construct $d$ commuting SYK Hamiltonians $\tilde H_a$ ($a=1,...,d$), each employing distinct commuting bases to ensure inter-group non-commutativity.

(b) $T_{r}<T_{0}$ and a pair of eigenvalues of $\tilde{\protect\mM}_{+}$ is imaginary (the right picture of (b)) though all eigenvalues of $\protect\mM_{+}$ are still real. (c) $T_{r}>T_{+}$ and all eigenvalues of $\protect\mM_{+}$ are real. (d) $T_{r}<T_{+}$ and a pair of eigenvalues of $\protect\mM_{+}$ is imaginary (the right picture of (d)).}

elements.sent-2411-701: Since this critical temperature is read from the exponent of the infinite exponential tail of the spectrum, we should naturally expect that there does not exist quantum chaos for $T\lessapprox T_c$ because the system simply does not have enough eigenvalues to scramble in the tail regime with a sparse spectrum.
elements.sent-2411-702: On the other hand, the existence of the $h=2$ mode for $q\rightarrow 1$, which is identical to the Schwarzian mode, suggests that quantum chaos should exist for $T\gtrapprox T_c$.
elements.sent-2411-703: This transition of quantum chaos will be further supported by our numerical studies of spectral form factor in Sec.. \ref{sec:SFF .}
elements.sent-2411-704: However, this physical interpretation has nothing to do with the critical temperature in the thermodynamical sense, defined as a non-smoothness change of free energy.

elements.sent-2411-709: Another diagonose of quantum chaos is the out-of-time-order correlators (OTOC).
elements.sent-2411-710: Since our model demonstrates a chaotic-integrable transition, it is interesting to check if we can observe similar transition in OTOC.
elements.sent-2411-711: On the other hand, since there are examples of OTOC overestimating the chaotic behavior of special models with unstable fixed points~\cite{Xu:2019lhc,Rozenbaum:2019nwn,Hashimoto:2020xfr,Trunin:2023xmw,Trunin:2023rwm , it is also interesting to test this in our model.}
